metadata.title: Hook vs. CLAUDE.md: Cannot, Not Do Not
metadata.description_blurb: CLAUDE.md said NEVER generate a final grade in capital letters — two sessions later Claude wrote 'Overall performance: B+' anyway. A PreToolUse hook makes that grade physically impossible to write to disk.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B00: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B06: palette=claude but the outro is 'un-annotated' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: The CLAUDE.md file said NEVER, in capital letters: NEVER generate a final grade. Two sessions later, Claude wrote 'Overall performance: B plus' in the student summary. Not because Claude ignored the rule. Because CLAUDE.md is advisory — Claude reads it and tries. A PreToolUse hook script does not try. It either passes or blocks. That is the difference. This is Liam, in for Bear.
beats.new_visual_element: ClaudeComposerAsk — cold open, student summary prompt

beats.narration_text: Claude weighted the NEVER instruction against the prompt's pull — 'summarize student performance' — and the prompt won. This is probabilistic behavior: Claude is a language model, not a rule engine. CLAUDE.md rules are intentions that Claude tries to follow. They are not constraints that the system enforces.
beats.new_visual_element: NEVER ignored — advisory vs deterministic table

beats.narration_text: A hook is a shell script that runs on a system event — before a file write, before a tool call. The PreToolUse hook receives a JSON payload on stdin describing what Claude is about to do. The script checks the payload. If it detects a grade pattern, it exits with a nonzero code. Claude Code sees that exit code and blocks the write. The file never changes.
beats.new_visual_element: Hook anatomy — PreToolUse / stdin JSON / exit 1 = blocked

beats.narration_text: You do not need to write the hook script. Ask Claude to write it. Describe the pattern to block — any grade letter followed by a plus or minus, any sentence containing 'overall performance' followed by a grade. Claude drafts the script. You review it — ten to fifteen minutes. You test it. If it works, you commit it.
beats.new_visual_element: Ask Claude to write the hook — teacher reviews and commits

beats.narration_text: Two layers. CLAUDE.md sets intent — the teacher's values, the advisory rules, the workflow context. The hook enforces the inviolable constraints — the rules that must hold regardless of prompt context. One layer is probabilistic. The other is deterministic. Use both. Do not conflate them.
beats.new_visual_element: Artifact view — CLAUDE.md layer + hook layer, two distinct enforcement levels

beats.narration_text: Write your first grade-blocking hook. Paste this prompt — Claude will draft the script from your pattern description.

props.command: Write a Claude Code PreToolUse hook script in bash that blocks any Write tool call where the content matches these patterns: any letter grade (A/B/C/D/F with optional +/-), or any phrase like 'overall performance' followed by a grade. The script should read stdin JSON, grep for the pattern, and exit 1 if matched. Include a test case.

beats.narration_text: If this turns your advisory rule into a hard constraint, subscribe. Liam, in for Bear.
